title: The Incident

jokes.text: The opening exchange where David and Patrick discover something wet in the bed — David's escalating panic vs. Patrick's calm confusion
jokes.setup: Couple wakes up to an undefined wet spot in their bed — could be anything innocuous
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire back-and-forth of 'what is it / what did you spill / are you bleeding' builds dread before the obvious conclusion is reached
jokes.explanation: The staccato pacing of the dialogue — each short line ratcheting up anxiety — is well-constructed. David's panic and Patrick's measured confusion are perfectly in character. The audience knows exactly where this is going but the writers delay the confirmation skillfully.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, there's something wet in the bed.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What? I didn't spill anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's not just on your side.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Are you suggesting that I—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not suggesting anything. I'm stating a fact.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I didn't have an accident.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Then what would you call this?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't know. Did you check if the dog got in the bed?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We don't have a dog, Patrick.

jokes.text: Do we have to file for divorce if we were never married?
jokes.setup: David has just wet the bed and is mortified beyond reason
jokes.punchline: His first instinct is to legally dissolve a relationship that doesn't yet legally exist
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. The joke works on multiple levels: it reveals David's catastrophizing personality, it's logically absurd yet completely in-character, and the legal framing of a non-legal relationship is a sharp comedic twist. Only David Rose would reach for divorce paperwork before looking for a towel. Ranks among the episode's best lines.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I need to tell you something. The wetness... it was me. I was so nervous about meeting your family that I actually... I sweated through my pants.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, no.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes. Profusely.

jokes.text: We are not getting divorced... Yet. I need to see if I can get the stain out of my sheets first.
jokes.setup: David proposes divorce; Patrick is calm
jokes.punchline: 'Yet' is the joke — Patrick doesn't rule out divorce, just deprioritizes it behind laundry. The 'yet' hangs perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's comedic persona is deadpan pragmatism against David's hysteria. 'Yet' is a single word that does enormous comedic work — it's both a joke and a character statement. The pivot to laundry logistics as the more pressing matter perfectly captures Patrick's grounded nature.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I cannot believe you told Patrick about the bed-wetting incident.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, it was one time, and it was years ago.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It doesn't matter. Now he knows I'm fundamentally broken.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're not broken. Everyone has embarrassing things.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Not like this. I'm leaving you.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What? Over this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. It's the only logical solution to my shame.

jokes.text: Okay, does that window open? Because I'm about to jump out of it!
jokes.setup: Patrick is calmly offering to help handle the situation
jokes.punchline: David's response is to consider defenestration as a viable exit strategy
jokes.explanation: Classic David hyperbole — functional, well-delivered, fits the escalation of his shame spiral. Not as sharp as the divorce line but a solid character beat. The 'asking permission to jump out' framing is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, if you're getting divorced, we should probably talk about the store. Like, who gets it? Because I've put a lot of work into that place.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I haven't even told Patrick yet.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Right, okay. Well, when you do, maybe lead with the store thing. Really softens the blow.

jokes.text: I will take a shower, but we must never see each other again.
jokes.setup: Patrick offers to deal with the sheets while David showers
jokes.punchline: David agrees to the practical arrangement while also demanding permanent estrangement as a condition
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of pragmatic acceptance (shower) with dramatic overreach (permanent separation) is pure David Rose. Very economical line — the comedy is entirely in the gap between 'yes to shower' and 'yes to never seeing you again.' Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Does this window open?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if it does, I'm jumping out of it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not staying in this town for another second. I'd rather die.

jokes.text: Okay, that sounds like a fair deal.
jokes.setup: David demands they never see each other again
jokes.punchline: Patrick's completely unbothered agreement, as if 'never see each other again' is a reasonable compromise
jokes.explanation: Five words of perfect deadpan. Patrick's comedic function as David's unflappable foil is never better expressed than here. The economy score is near-perfect — not a syllable wasted.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to help. Maybe we could look at some more affordable options?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Affordable? Patrick, I don't think you understand. This is a catastrophe of biblical proportions. My entire life as I know it is over.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's just a budget adjustment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Just a budget adjustment? JUST? Do you have any idea what you're asking of me? I might have to shop at a store where they don't know my name. Where they don't greet me by my preferred pronouns. This is social death, Patrick. I'm as good as gone.

jokes.text: I love you. / I'm glad one of us does!
jokes.setup: Patrick says 'I love you' as David is shuffling to the shower in shame
jokes.punchline: David's bitter, self-loathing response — he currently does not love himself
jokes.explanation: A genuinely lovely two-line exchange that's simultaneously funny and touching. Patrick's warmth cuts through the chaos; David's response is self-deprecating humor at its most character-specific. The fact that it's also heartfelt gives it rewatch value. One of the episode's standout moments.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I love you. I love you so much.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's very sweet, but I don't think you understand what you're saying. I'm a very difficult person. I'm selfish, I'm vain, I'm judgmental...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, you don't. You really don't.

jokes.text: So how was your seventh shower? Satisfactory, thank you so much.
jokes.setup: Time has passed; David has been showering obsessively
jokes.punchline: 'Seventh' is the specific number that makes it funny — not two or three, but seven. David's prim 'satisfactory' is the perfect overly-composed response.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'seventh' is good craft — it implies the obsessive shame spiral without showing it. David's overly dignified response is very in character for someone trying to reclaim composure after a deeply undignified event.

jokes.text: Things happen all in your sheets, and now you need to remake your little bed, because I peed in it!
jokes.setup: Patrick tries to normalize the incident with 'things happen'
jokes.punchline: David takes Patrick's euphemism and blows it apart with total specificity — no, these are not abstract 'things,' I peed in your bed
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the way David destroys Patrick's gentle management strategy by naming the thing explicitly. His horrified specificity undercuts every attempt to soften the situation. The 'little bed' detail is a nice character dig too.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, just talk to me. What's going on?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't want to discuss this.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We need to talk about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There is nothing to talk about.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. I cried during the opening credits of a baking show. Are you happy?

jokes.text: I found this blanket that I thought I had lost in the laundry room, so you've actually done me a favour.
jokes.setup: Patrick is trying every angle to make David feel better about the incident
jokes.punchline: He reframes David wetting the bed as a net positive because it led to finding a lost blanket
jokes.explanation: Patrick's relentlessly optimistic reframing is a good comedic strategy — every silver lining he finds is more absurd than the last. 'You've actually done me a favour' applied to bed-wetting is a funny inversion of logic that's very Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is so embarrassing. You're going to think I'm disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I don't think that at all. Look, everyone has accidents. At least now we know your body is still capable of surprises.

jokes.text: The crinkling sound discovery — David realizes there's a mattress protector on the bed
jokes.setup: David is sitting on the freshly remade bed when he notices a strange crinkling sound. Patrick attributes it innocuously to the mattress tag.
jokes.punchline: David pulls back the sheet to reveal a plastic mattress protector — Patrick had it there all along
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully structured comedic reveal. The audience suspects what's under the sheet before David does, creating dramatic irony. 'It's just the mattress tag' is a textbook misdirection that holds just long enough. The visual of David discovering the plastic sheet is the real punchline — this is physical/visual comedy executed through structure. It also recontextualizes Patrick's earlier calm, revealing premeditation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate Patrick's generosity, but let's be honest, I would never let that go. I would bring it up constantly. 'Oh, you want to go out tonight? Remember when you wet the bed?'

jokes.text: I just wanted you to be comfortable in case it happened again... Come on, David, it's an expensive mattress!
jokes.setup: David is outraged that Patrick preemptively put down a mattress protector
jokes.punchline: Patrick's second justification drops the pretense of concern for David and reveals pure materialism — 'it's an expensive mattress'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I care about you' to 'I care about my mattress' is funny because Patrick is normally so selfless. The honesty of 'it's an expensive mattress' punctures his carefully constructed sympathy and makes him briefly the butt of the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, is that... did you prepare for this?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You bought crinkly pants. On purpose. You planned ahead for a moment where you might need to deny something loudly.

jokes.text: More expensive than my dignity? / I mean, comparable, at least.
jokes.setup: David is indignant about the mattress protector being prioritized over his dignity
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'comparable, at least' suggests the mattress and David's dignity are in the same price range
jokes.explanation: This is the comedic peak of the opening sequence. 'Comparable, at least' is a ruthlessly precise line — it doesn't say the mattress is MORE valuable than David's dignity, just roughly equivalent. The qualification 'at least' makes it even funnier, implying Patrick considered saying it was more expensive but settled on parity. Economy score is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I just like to sleep on a towel. It's very absorbent. Great for moisture.

jokes.text: Takeover? That sounds hostile.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains that Interflix wants Moira to do a 'social media takeover'
jokes.punchline: Moira interprets 'takeover' through a dramatic/political lens rather than a marketing one
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Moira's unfamiliarity with social media terminology read through her theatrical worldview. Quick and efficient but not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, what are you doing? You're preparing the salmon like you're performing surgery.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, if we're going to serve it, we should do it properly. Besides, you never know when a fish bone could become a liability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's dinner, not a courtroom.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Exactly. Which is why we prepare it correctly.

jokes.text: I've dated enough mid-level latte art influencers to know what the people want.
jokes.setup: Alexis is claiming expertise in social media
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of 'mid-level latte art influencers' as a dating demographic implies a very particular social stratum of Alexis's past
jokes.explanation: The word 'mid-level' is doing enormous work here — it's not just 'influencers,' it's the stratification within that world that reveals Alexis's knowledge of and immersion in influencer culture. This is highly character-specific: no one else on this show could deliver this line. The casual confidence makes it funnier. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that my dignity is worth less than a mattress?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, a good mattress can last you ten years...

jokes.text: I'm sorry Alexis, I only understood about half of what you just said.
jokes.setup: Alexis has rattled off social media jargon
jokes.punchline: Moira's regal admission of incomprehension — not embarrassed, just reporting a fact
jokes.explanation: Standard fish-out-of-water beat — Moira doesn't understand modern media. Works for the character but is a fairly expected joke given the setup. Functional rather than inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So basically, a corporation is taking over my social media accounts for the day to show their followers what I do.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how wonderful! A hostile takeover of your digital presence. I had one of those once. Very traumatic. They took my wig collection and my entire skincare line.

jokes.text: I take a slightly more cerebral approach. / Okay, you were the one that described social media as an amusement park for clinical narcissists.
jokes.setup: Moira claims she has superior comedic sensibility
jokes.punchline: Alexis reminds her of her own withering insult of social media, undercutting Moira's current enthusiasm for it
jokes.explanation: 'An amusement park for clinical narcissists' is a great Moira-ism — the kind of ornate insult she'd use. Alexis wielding it against her is good comedic structure. The irony of Moira criticizing narcissism while being Moira is layered.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I basically invented Instagram. Well, not invented, but I was definitely an early adopter. I had like 50,000 followers before it was even a thing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That doesn't make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well I dated a guy who worked at Facebook, so I basically knew Mark Zuckerberg. And then I dated his roommate, who was a photographer, so that's basically Instagram. And then I dated an influencer before influencers were even a thing. So yeah, I invented social media.

jokes.text: Big news coming soon, caw caw! — with a yellow winking emoji
jokes.setup: Moira claimed she has a more cerebral comedic approach; Alexis is ghostwriting her social media
jokes.punchline: Alexis's idea of Moira's voice is 'caw caw' and a cartoon winking emoji — maximally undignified
jokes.explanation: The gap between Moira's self-image and Alexis's representation of her is the joke. 'Caw caw' is a funny callback to crow movie marketing. The winking emoji as the punchline visual is a nice touch for the medium.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't do slapstick. I'm more of a physical comedian in the vein of Greta Garbo or a silent film ingénue who communicates through meaningful glances and the strategic placement of her hands.

jokes.text: You know what would be fun, Alexis, let's put a beak on that winking cartoon.
jokes.setup: Alexis shows Moira the drafted post with a winking emoji
jokes.punchline: Moira's idea of improving it is adding a beak — she wants to make the emoji more crow-like with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is Moira at her most Moira — engaging earnestly with the wrong aspect of the problem. She's not wrong that a crow emoji would be more thematic, but the specificity of 'put a beak on that winking cartoon' is delightfully absurd. Highly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Social media is just a series of portals for people to project their curated delusions onto an audience of strangers who are equally invested in maintaining their own fictional narratives.

jokes.text: Alexis, this is exciting! We have our very own digital soapbox here! It reminds me of the Nickelodeon pilot I did in which Ashley Tisdale and I played suffragettes. You remember, 'You Go, Girl.'
jokes.setup: Moira is getting enthusiastic about social media
jokes.punchline: She free-associates to a Nickelodeon pilot with Ashley Tisdale where they played suffragettes, titled 'You Go, Girl' — a cascade of absurd specificity
jokes.explanation: The Moira Rose fake-filmography jokes are always high-quality because each one is a tiny world — a Nickelodeon pilot about suffragettes starring Ashley Tisdale, titled 'You Go, Girl,' is three separate absurd ideas colliding. The detail that she just casually had a Nickelodeon pilot and a celebrity co-star from that era is perfectly calibrated Moira mythology.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I'm thinking for the crow movie, we do a really fun, engaging social media post. Something like... 'CAW CAW! This movie is gonna be LIT! Get ready to have your minds BLOWN by these sexy crows!' With like, a bunch of emojis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, no.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? It's fun! It's relatable! Crows are having a moment!

jokes.text: People are cawing back! Oh Alexis, I think you and I might need to purchase... antibiotics. I believe we've just gone viral.
jokes.setup: Moira is watching likes roll in on the Interflix post
jokes.punchline: Moira confuses/conflates 'going viral' (internet) with an actual infection requiring antibiotics, then delivers 'gone viral' as if she's just invented the phrase
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The pivot through 'antibiotics' is a perfectly constructed misdirection — for one beat, the audience thinks Moira has genuinely confused the concepts — and then 'gone viral' lands as her triumphant declaration. The pause before 'antibiotics' is crucial structural work. It's a Moira-ism that has already become iconic in the fandom. Character-specific comedy of the highest order.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, darling, your emoji usage is positively pedestrian. You simply must elevate your digital communication.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I think I'm fine with emojis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Nonsense. Here, I've composed a message using only the most sophisticated emojis. *reads from phone* Eggplant, peach, water droplets, followed by a microphone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, no!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? I'm simply describing last night's ratatouille. It was followed by a rather impressive vocal performance.

jokes.text: A 20 minute drive to another motel. [scene establishes Johnny, Stevie, and Roland arriving at what appears to be a rundown property]
jokes.setup: Johnny has been hyping up a new motel opportunity
jokes.punchline: The visual of the motel itself is presumably underwhelming — a 20-minute drive to something modest enough to require Stevie's skeptical 'uh'
jokes.explanation: A functional scene-setting joke. The '20 minute drive' framing of the reveal is mildly comic but primarily expository. Stevie's 'uh' reaction beat carries most of the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once did a historical drama about the French Revolution called 'Let Them Eat Spray Tan.'

jokes.text: No surprise there, Johnny, I told you she's not the visionary you and I are.
jokes.setup: Stevie expresses skepticism about buying the motel
jokes.punchline: Roland positions himself as a 'visionary' alongside Johnny — the absurdity of Roland claiming visionary status is the joke
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-aggrandizement is a reliable character beat. The comedy comes entirely from the audience knowing Roland is definitively not a visionary. Works because of prior character establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Five likes? FIVE?! Do you understand what this means? I have officially gone viral. I am a social media sensation. *This* is how it starts. Tomorrow, sponsorships. Next week, a Netflix deal. I'm already mentally preparing my acceptance speech for Influencer of the Year.

jokes.text: Huh, Betty and Benny? What are they, puppets? It's the Benny and Betty Show! / Tu! Tu! / I'm Betty. I'm Benny. I'm Betty!
jokes.setup: The agent mentions that Betty is Benny's wife
jokes.punchline: Roland does an impromptu puppet show impression, complete with voices, in front of the bereaved widow's agent
jokes.explanation: Roland doing spontaneous puppet theater at an inappropriate moment is very him. The 'Tu! Tu!' puppet sound effects and his switching between Betty and Benny voices is physical comedy rendered in text. The inappropriateness (they're about to enter a funeral) is part of what makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We've gone viral.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, that's not what that means.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't appreciate your tone, David. I'm clearly very ill. I need to see a specialist immediately. Perhaps someone from Johns Hopkins?

jokes.text: Why do I get the feeling this isn't an open house? / Maybe it's because of the coffin. This is a funeral, Roland!
jokes.setup: The trio has just walked into the Terkstra property, sensing something is off
jokes.punchline: Stevie deadpans the obvious: there's a coffin. Johnny's 'This is a funeral, Roland!' is the real explosion.
jokes.explanation: The delayed reveal that they've walked into a funeral is a great structural moment. Stevie's understated 'maybe it's because of the coffin' is perfectly deadpan, and Johnny's furious 'This is a FUNERAL, Roland!' pays off the entire setup sequence beautifully. The word 'Roland' doing double duty as both name and condemnation is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know if buying another motel is the right move for me.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? You'd be lucky to have my business acumen rub off on you. I've made some very shrewd real estate decisions.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You literally lost your motel.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly. Strategic pivot.

jokes.text: She may have said viewing.
jokes.setup: Johnny demands to know what Roland was told, having just realized they've crashed a funeral
jokes.punchline: Roland's quiet, almost apologetic admission that 'viewing' and 'showing' sound somewhat similar to him
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly explain the entire disaster while being maximally funny. The understatement of 'may have' is brilliant — Roland isn't even certain he misunderstood. The economy is near-perfect; this line could not be shorter without losing the joke. The almost-rhyme of 'viewing/showing' makes the error just plausible enough to be funny rather than impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Betty and Benny, they sound awfully similar. Reminds me of my puppet show days.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Betty! And I'm Benny! We're here to teach you about proper business etiquette!

jokes.text: What the hell, Roland?! / Johnny, language, please! A man is dead.
jokes.setup: Johnny explodes at Roland for leading them to a funeral
jokes.punchline: Roland's rebuke is not 'I'm sorry' but rather shushing Johnny for swearing — in his mind, the profanity is the more pressing breach of etiquette
jokes.explanation: 'A man is dead' as Roland's moral trump card — not to acknowledge his error, but to police Johnny's language — is a very sharp comedic inversion. Roland's sense of priority is profoundly scrambled in a way that's completely in character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, this is quite the turnout for an open house.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's just us.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Where is everyone?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Johnny, this isn't an open house. This is my apartment. You're just here to help me move furniture.

jokes.text: It's a little word I assembled to consecrate my fans, who are also my friends.
jokes.setup: Moira introduces her neologism 'frands'
jokes.punchline: The word 'consecrate' for a social media fan portmanteau — a word suggesting religious sanctification applied to internet followers
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary choices are consistently a source of comedy — 'assembled' and 'consecrate' applied to a fan-friend portmanteau is very Moira. The grandiosity of the language versus the silliness of the concept is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: She said she was showing at 9 o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh wait, she said viewing. I heard showing.

jokes.text: Okay, 'frands' doesn't sound nice.
jokes.setup: Moira explains 'frands' as fans who are also friends
jokes.punchline: David's flat, honest assessment cuts through Moira's enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: David's reaction beat is well-timed — 'doesn't sound nice' is a funny but accurate observation delivered with his characteristic flat affect. The economy is great; any more words would kill it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, what were you thinking? You've completely ruined everything!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now Johnny, I can assure you, I am a man of the utmost integrity and professionalism.

jokes.text: Well, it so happens it's also owned by my son, David Rose, say hi, David. / Okay, I would rather not, thanks.
jokes.setup: Moira is livestreaming in the store and addresses the camera audience about the shop
jokes.punchline: David's flat refusal to perform for the camera — his mortification expressed as polite declination
jokes.explanation: David's 'I would rather not, thanks' is a perfectly civil way to express deep reluctance. The 'thanks' is doing a lot of funny work — he's declining as if it's a reasonable request that he's considered and rejected. Functional character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You haven't touched your tea.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know. I'm just... I'm doing some reorganizing here. Very important work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These color swatches need to be... rearranged by shade.

jokes.text: And his hashtag fiancee, Patrick. / I don't think you have to say hashtag when you're just talking, Mrs. Rose.
jokes.setup: Moira is narrating for her social media audience but is confusing on-screen text formatting with spoken language
jokes.punchline: Patrick's gentle correction that hashtags are a visual medium, not a verbal one
jokes.explanation: Moira saying 'hashtag fiancee' out loud is a funny illustration of her total misunderstanding of social media mechanics. Patrick's correction is the straight-man reaction done well — matter-of-fact, not condescending.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, a portmanteau is when you combine two words to create a new word with a blended meaning. It's a device used by the most eloquent among us.

jokes.text: Okay, what are you doing? You look like the downfall of society.
jokes.setup: Moira is livestreaming in their shop, clearly making a spectacle
jokes.punchline: David's insult is maximally dramatic — not 'you look embarrassing,' but 'the downfall of society'
jokes.explanation: David's hyperbolic insult language is always fun. 'The downfall of society' for someone livestreaming in a gift shop is perfectly calibrated over-the-top disapproval. It's also character-consistent with his earlier social media critique.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not going to use that word.

jokes.text: Frands, please keep checking your phones for more 'Crow' related intel by me, Moira Rose, especially you, 'Wine and Cat Gal 74,' I do hope you stop crying soon. Caw! Caw! For now. Muah!
jokes.setup: Moira is signing off from her store appearance
jokes.punchline: The specific call-out of 'Wine and Cat Gal 74' with genuine concern for their emotional wellbeing, followed by 'Caw! Caw! For now. Muah!' as a sign-off
jokes.explanation: Moira personally addressing 'Wine and Cat Gal 74' with genuine emotional concern is both absurd and somehow sweet — it's Moira being unexpectedly human in the most bizarre way possible. The 'Caw! Caw! For now. Muah!' sign-off is a perfect Moira-branded farewell. The running gag of Wine and Cat Gal 74 is efficiently established here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, say hi to social media.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Hm, it's noticeably cheerless in here today. If I didn't know better, I would suspect a little trouble in paradise.
jokes.setup: David is visibly miserable in the store
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical observation frames David's shame spiral as a relationship drama she can appreciate
jokes.explanation: Functional observation that advances the plot — Moira notices something is off and is fishing. 'Trouble in paradise' is a predictable idiom choice for this context, which keeps the score modest.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Hashtag fiancée.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, actually, it would be 'hashtag fiancé' since you're marrying a man.

jokes.text: David's just having a bit of a day. Didn't get a lot of sleep last night.
jokes.setup: Moira suspects trouble; Patrick knows the truth
jokes.punchline: Patrick's magnificently understated deflection — 'a bit of a day' and 'didn't get a lot of sleep' for a bed-wetting incident
jokes.explanation: Patrick's loyalty expressed as masterclass understatement. 'Didn't get a lot of sleep' is technically accurate but maximally deflecting. The audience gets to enjoy knowing the truth while watching the polite fiction maintained.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're not going to get any followers by filming yourself in a dark room talking to your phone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I have to start somewhere.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and that somewhere is not here, doing that.

jokes.text: Your sleep apnea hasn't returned, has it? I thought that was remedied when you got your new nose.
jokes.setup: Patrick says David didn't sleep well; Moira tries to diagnose
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual revelation that David has had a nose job, delivered as a throwaway medical history detail — 'his new nose'
jokes.explanation: The revelation of David's rhinoplasty history as a casual, uncontested fact dropped into medical concern is wonderful. Moira's comfort with this information — the matter-of-fact nature of 'his new nose' — is very funny. It's also a good example of the show's world-building through comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oddly specific and personal message to random username

jokes.text: Is it night sweats? You're not eating pepperettes in bed again!
jokes.setup: Moira is running through possible diagnoses for David's bad night
jokes.punchline: 'Pepperettes in bed' as a specific concern — implying this is a known habit that requires Moira's warning
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'pepperettes' (a type of meat snack) rather than generic snacks is the key craft choice here. It implies a whole history of David's in-bed snacking that Moira has knowledge of. The escalation from sleep apnea to night sweats to pepperette consumption is a funny diagnostic spiral.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, David. I can see the tension between you two. It's like a Chekhov play, but with better lighting and a more forgiving color palette.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're both just standing there, festering in your own emotional quicksand. It's very Russian, very bleak, very you.

jokes.text: David, don't tell me you've had a nighttime oopsie-daisy.
jokes.setup: David refuses to explain what's wrong; Moira is guessing
jokes.punchline: Moira's euphemism for bed-wetting — 'nighttime oopsie-daisy' — is simultaneously childlike and Moira-dramatic
jokes.explanation: 'Nighttime oopsie-daisy' is a perfect Moira coinage — it sounds like something a Victorian nanny would say, and its delicacy is in sharp contrast to what it's describing. The 'don't tell me' before it builds the anticipation perfectly. This is a standout Moira line and highly quotable. Only Catherine O'Hara/Moira Rose could deliver this exact phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Has David mentioned anything about his sleep apnea? I've been reading about it, and apparently it can be quite serious. Though I suppose with his nose job, he should be breathing better than ever.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, a what? / I'm leaving. Okay? And if I don't come back it's because of you.
jokes.setup: Moira guesses David had a 'nighttime oopsie-daisy'; Patrick didn't know Moira knew this term
jokes.punchline: Patrick's stunned 'I'm sorry, a what?' is the straight-man reaction; David immediately blames Patrick for existing in this moment
jokes.explanation: Patrick's 'I'm sorry, a what?' is an excellent reaction beat — he's heard the term but can't believe it. David blaming Patrick ('it's because of you') for his own decision to leave is very David: redirect all shame outward.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I've been researching your sleep issues. Tell me, what do you eat before bed?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Nothing unusual. Maybe some toast.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Toast? David, I've discovered the problem. You're consuming carbohydrates after sunset. It's causing your circadian rhythms to misalign with the lunar cycles.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've also noticed you've been having a little midnight snack of cold cuts and marshmallow fluff directly from the jar with your fingers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have not.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I found the jar under your bed with finger marks that match your hand span. It's oddly specific, even for you.

jokes.text: David's nocturnal enuresis used to only happen when he went to bed all juiced up with excited anticipation about something. Christmas, birthdays, the Ides of March.
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining David's bed-wetting history to Patrick
jokes.punchline: The list of David's excitements escalates normally (Christmas, birthdays) and then ends with 'the Ides of March' — an intensely specific and historically ominous date that David apparently found thrilling
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. The list-joke structure builds with two entirely normal items and then pivots to 'the Ides of March' — a date associated with Julius Caesar's assassination. The joke works on every level: it's character-specific (only a Rose family child would get excited about the Ides of March), it implies a deeply unusual upbringing, it subverts the list structure perfectly, and it rewards cultural literacy without requiring it. The delivery is straight, which makes it funnier. This is Schitt's Creek writing at its peak — absurdist but completely grounded in character. Approaches the calibration anchor of 9.5 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you need to stop having your little accidents in the night.

jokes.text: And now it seems your impending nuptials has opened the floodgates.
jokes.setup: Moira has explained David's history of excitement-induced bed-wetting
jokes.punchline: 'Opened the floodgates' is a perfectly chosen idiom — it means 'released a flood of emotion' but also literally describes what happened to David
jokes.explanation: An absolutely perfect double-meaning. 'Floodgates' used in the context of emotional release + literal urination is a wordplay joke that succeeds because it doesn't announce itself — Moira says it with complete sincerity as if she's using the idiom conventionally, leaving the audience to catch the double meaning a beat later. This is the kind of writing that looks easy and isn't.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David had a condition called nocturnal enuresis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a medical term, David. Though I suppose you could say he was quite juiced up at night.

jokes.text: Why am I oddly flattered? / I can't imagine.
jokes.setup: Moira has explained that David's bed-wetting means he's excited about the upcoming wedding
jokes.punchline: Patrick's charmed response — he's somehow touched by this information; Moira's 'I can't imagine' is dry but warm
jokes.explanation: Patrick's 'oddly flattered' is funny because it's true — discovering your partner's bed-wetting means they're excited to marry you IS oddly romantic. His ability to find the sweet interpretation of every David situation is core to his character. Moira's dry 'I can't imagine' is a perfect button.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, there was the time he got a new duvet cover, and a particularly well-organized linen closet, and apparently the day they announced the Treaty of Westphalia.

jokes.text: From the bowling league. / Uh, you mean curling?
jokes.setup: Johnny is improvising how he knew Benny; Roland tries to help by naming a sport
jokes.punchline: Roland gets the sport wrong — there is no bowling league, there was apparently a curling league, which Johnny didn't know about either
jokes.explanation: Roland volunteering the wrong information while trying to help is a good comedic escalation of their already-bad situation. The specific substitution of bowling for curling (both somewhat obscure activities) is a nice choice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David has always had a problem with his floodgates. Even as a child, the slightest bit of excitement and suddenly we're mopping up the carpet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm simply saying that you've never been one to contain your emotions, darling.

jokes.text: Well, that's because Johnny here... had another bonspiel to go to, and unfortunately that bonspiel was way more important than Benny's bonspiel, so we had to go to that bonspiel.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Terkstra can't place Johnny at Benny's last curling bonspiel
jokes.punchline: Roland attempts to cover for Johnny by inventing a hierarchy of bonspiels — the word 'bonspiel' repeated three times becoming funnier with each repetition
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'bonspiel' — an obscure enough word that three uses in one sentence feel ridiculous — is the engine of this joke. Roland's logic (this bonspiel was 'way more important' than Benny's) implies a bonspiel pecking order that is both absurd and very Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's actually kind of romantic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How is that romantic?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Because you're so excited about marrying me that you literally can't control yourself.

jokes.text: Since I wasn't in the curling league, can I go home? / No. No.
jokes.setup: Roland and Johnny are deeply entangled in a lie about being in Benny's curling league
jokes.punchline: Stevie, who has no stake in this lie, reasonably asks to be excused; Johnny refuses without explanation
jokes.explanation: Stevie's pragmatic 'can I go home?' is perfectly Stevie — she sees no reason she should suffer for Roland's incompetence. Johnny's flat 'No. No.' (repeated for emphasis) is funny in its arbitrary authority. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I've learned that life is like a box of donuts. You never know what you're gonna get, but you're always gonna want more.

jokes.text: Say Havarti, girls! [Moira tries to take a photo with the Jazzigals]
jokes.setup: Moira has been doing a social media takeover and wants a photo with her singing group
jokes.punchline: 'Say Havarti' instead of 'say cheese' — a Moira substitution that implies she finds cheese too common but a specific artisanal cheese is refined enough
jokes.explanation: Another perfect Moira-ism. The fact that she specifically chose Havarti — a soft, mild Danish cheese that still sounds fancy — over 'cheese' reveals an entire character philosophy in two words. Only Moira would upgrade the photo instruction.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny had to step out, but he's very much into the bonspiel. Very into it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He talks about bonspiels all the time. Different bonspiels.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been to like, five bonspiels with him. Maybe six bonspiels.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He loves the bonspiel. The bonspieling. All of it.

jokes.text: Oh no! Well, you can't win them all.
jokes.setup: The photo/video clearly didn't turn out well — implied from the reaction
jokes.punchline: Alexis's completely unbothered 'you can't win them all' suggests she had low expectations from the start
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat. The comedy is mostly in Alexis's inappropriate nonchalance — her standards are so low she's already written this off. Not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't curl. I don't know anything about curling. And I'm not going to stand here and pretend that I do.

jokes.text: Wow, someone's in a surprisingly good mood, considering they're 15 minutes late.
jokes.setup: Moira has arrived late and is in a great mood from her social media success
jokes.punchline: Patrick's understated passive-aggressive note about lateness — 'surprisingly good mood' is the key ironic qualifier
jokes.explanation: Standard mild-passive-aggression joke. Works for Patrick's character (he runs a business and cares about punctuality) but is fairly forgettable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Who taught you how to livestream?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A very sweet girl from Belarus. And before you ask, yes, I did try to adopt her.

jokes.text: What did you call it, a um, a cauldron of self-absorption.
jokes.setup: Moira claims she hated social media; Twyla recalls her own words
jokes.punchline: 'A cauldron of self-absorption' — we learn this is what Moira previously called social media, now revealed as she's deep into loving it
jokes.explanation: 'Cauldron of self-absorption' is a great Moira phrase — gothic, dramatic, specific. Twyla's recall of it lands the irony: Moira condemned it and has now embraced it. The joke also characterizes Twyla as a better listener than people give her credit for.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So basically, my mom accidentally broadcast David's private moment to like thousands of people on her podcast.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It wasn't just a moment, Alexis. It was a full four minutes of me having an emotional breakdown in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, the listeners seemed really engaged with it. Mom's getting her best download numbers ever.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's called 'content,' David. The people want authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The people want my humiliation? That's not authenticity, that's exploitation!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Look on the bright side — you're basically famous now. People recognize you at the grocery store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, as 'the guy who cried about his skincare routine on a podcast.' That's exactly the legacy I was going for.

jokes.text: You have an almost unsetting memory, Twyla.
jokes.setup: Twyla has accurately recalled Moira's past condemnation of social media
jokes.punchline: Moira calls it 'unsetting' rather than 'unsettling' — a Moira malapropism that suggests she means the memory is like a thing that can't be un-set, while trying to say it disturbs her
jokes.explanation: Moira's malapropisms are a show signature, and 'unsetting' for 'unsettling' is a good one — it's close enough that it sounds like a word that could exist, but it isn't quite right. The affectation of using an unusual word to be sophisticated while getting it slightly wrong is peak Moira. Rewatch bonus because attentive viewers catch it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I think we've stopped recording.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, finally. So anyway, I've been thinking about getting a boob job.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, don't be ridiculous. You're beautiful just as you are.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Really? Because David said they were a bit... droopy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David's an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Radio Host
dialogue.line: Folks, we're still live. That was Moira Rose, everyone.

jokes.text: Now just a quick snap for my friend, 'Wine and Cat Gal 74,' and her frands at the rehabilitation facility.
jokes.setup: Moira wants to take a photo for her online followers
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that Wine and Cat Gal 74 is at a rehabilitation facility — Moira says this as a neutral fact while planning a cheerful photo for her
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Wine and Cat Gal 74 deepens here — her being at a rehabilitation facility, with 'Wine' in her username, is a perfectly dark detail that Moira mentions without registering its implications. The contrast between Moira's cheerful obliviousness and the implied subtext is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know I should be more concerned about all of this, but honestly, I'm still kind of enjoying the disaster.

jokes.text: Who taught you how to livestream?! / A wonderful teen named CherryBus, she lives in Singapore. Why? Who wants to know?
jokes.setup: Alexis discovers Moira has been livestreaming without knowing it
jokes.punchline: Moira's social media education came from a teenager named 'CherryBus' in Singapore — and her defensive 'Why? Who wants to know?' suggests she's proud of this mentorship
jokes.explanation: 'CherryBus' as a screen name/username is a funny piece of specificity. The idea of Moira Rose, grande dame of Canadian theater, being tutored in digital media by a Singaporean teenager is a great image. Her defensive 'Why? Who wants to know?' about her internet mentor is a very funny beat.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Terkstra
dialogue.line: You look like federal agents.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're not federal agents.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Terkstra
dialogue.line: Well, she does.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Terkstra
dialogue.line: Look at her. She's obviously law enforcement.

jokes.text: So people basically got like, a whole podcast about how David's wetting the bed again.
jokes.setup: Moira accidentally livestreamed her conversation in the store, including the discussion of David's incident
jokes.punchline: Alexis's deadpan summary of the catastrophe as 'basically a whole podcast about how David's wetting the bed again' — the word 'again' doing heavy work
jokes.explanation: The callback earns its place because it compounds the disaster. 'Again' implies an established pattern that is now public knowledge. The framing as 'a podcast' is a good modern media joke. Alexis's calm assessment of maximum damage is very her.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Maureen and I go way back. We met at a curling bonspiel in '98. She was skip on her team, I was throwing fourth rocks. We didn't talk much that day, but our eyes met across the sheet, you know? And I thought, 'That's a woman who knows her way around a rock.'

jokes.text: If I wasn't your publicist, I would be enjoying the situation a lot more than I am. Which is still like, quite a lot.
jokes.setup: Moira has accidentally broadcast David's embarrassing secret to the internet
jokes.punchline: Alexis admits she's still enjoying this quite a lot even in her professional capacity — her sisterly schadenfreude leaking through
jokes.explanation: The two-part structure is excellent — the first part (professional restraint) sets up the second (she's still enjoying it quite a lot). 'Which is still like, quite a lot' is a great self-correction that reveals her actual emotional state. Very Alexis: aware enough to acknowledge professional conflict of interest, not restrained enough to suppress the pleasure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, a viewing? Well, I'd be delighted to pay my respects. What time should I arrive? And, uh, will there be refreshments?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I do love a good open casket. Really gives you a chance to see what you're working with.

jokes.text: Fortunately, David doesn't subscribe to social media, so we just need to take it down, and David will be none the wiser.
jokes.setup: The plan to hide the livestream disaster from David seems viable
jokes.punchline: The audience knows this plan will probably fail — it's announced too confidently to succeed
jokes.explanation: Functional dramatic irony setup. The audience can predict this plan will unravel. Not particularly inventive but necessary for plot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My social media mentor has the most ridiculous username. I mean, it's completely absurd. But I have to treat it as a formal address every time I see her. 'Hello, @SparklePrincess2847, how are you today?' It's absolutely exhausting.

jokes.text: Hey Moira, you weren't lying! You do have a knack for this.
jokes.setup: The group is in crisis mode about the livestream; Twyla has clearly been watching it
jokes.punchline: Twyla's chipper compliment, delivered as if the content of the livestream was just good entertainment, is tone-deaf in the best way
jokes.explanation: Twyla's gentle obliviousness is a good character beat here. Her compliment is sincere, which makes it the wrong thing to say, which makes it funny. Consistent with her character of being kind but occasionally missing the emotional temperature of a room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We need to keep this on a need-to-know basis. David does not need to know. What he doesn't know won't hurt him, and what he does know will definitely hurt him.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're going to treat this like a covert operation. Code names, encrypted messages, dead drops in the town square.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've already secured the perimeter. Twyla is stationed at the front desk. Jazzagals have been notified. Radio silence on all fronts.

jokes.text: Okay, um, no need to watch that. Okay, Twyla, if you haven't seen it already, then maybe just once, but that is it!
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to contain the damage from the livestream
jokes.punchline: Her own irresistible curiosity/rubbernecking undermines her crisis management — she immediately carves out an exception for Twyla to watch 'just once'
jokes.explanation: Alexis caught between professional duty and personal desire to let Twyla watch is a funny character inconsistency. The immediate backpedal from 'no need to watch' to 'just once' is a good comedic reversal. Shows her character: she cares about David in theory but finds his humiliation somewhat enjoyable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I left the button factory as a young man.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, technically I was a child when I left, so I suppose that makes me a child laborer.

jokes.text: Her face has 'fed' written all over it.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Terkstra has mistaken the Rosebud group for federal agents
jokes.punchline: She singles out Stevie as looking like a federal agent — Stevie's default deadpan expression apparently reads as law enforcement
jokes.explanation: The idea that Stevie's face just naturally reads as 'federal agent' is both absurd and somehow plausible given the character's deadpan energy. Stevie's 'Thanks...' reaction is a perfect beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I can see the enthusiasm just radiating off of you.

jokes.text: What a small world! / Johnny and I used to play bingo with Maureen, right after curling. Roland.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Terkstra reveals that Benny and she used to play bingo with Maureen from Rosebud Motel, connecting the dots
jokes.punchline: Roland enthusiastically adds invented details ('right after curling') to their fictional relationship with Maureen, then Johnny says his name as a one-word rebuke
jokes.explanation: Johnny saying 'Roland.' as a complete sentence — a name used as a rebuke — is very economical comedy. Roland keeps adding details to their lie (curling again!) and Johnny's one-word response encapsulates exhausted exasperation perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm so glad you decided to stay for the evening.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I would have left, but I'm severely dehydrated from the embarrassment, and I didn't think it would be wise to operate a vehicle in this condition.

jokes.text: Too bad it's not an open casket. I would've loved to say goodbye to old Benny's face.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Terkstra invites them to pay their respects; the casket is closed
jokes.punchline: Roland's genuine disappointment at not seeing the corpse — he didn't know Benny at all but has fully committed to the emotional performance of the grieving friend
jokes.explanation: 'Old Benny's face' as if Roland has fond memories of a man he never met — the familiarity of the phrasing against the reality of total fabrication is darkly funny. Roland's ability to perform genuine-feeling sentiment for someone he invented a relationship with is impressive and unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are we seriously discussing the thread count of plastic sheeting right now?

jokes.text: We'd be happy to open it for you. / No, no, closed-closed is fine. You sure?
jokes.setup: Roland expresses his wish to see Benny's face; Mrs. Terkstra offers to open the casket
jokes.punchline: Johnny's panicked 'No, no, closed-closed is fine' — the doubled 'closed' indicating how firmly he does not want that casket opened; Mrs. Terkstra's 'You sure?' pressing the offer
jokes.explanation: The dark escalation to 'we could open the casket' is unexpected and plays on the absurdity of the situation. Johnny's 'closed-closed' (the repetition is the joke) communicates desperate certainty. Mrs. Terkstra pressing with 'You sure?' is a lovely final beat.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I sleep with a humidifier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's very practical.

jokes.text: And David is bewitching himself elsewhere? / Yeah, he is uh, dusting the shelves, even though I cleaned them last week, but hey, whatever it takes to get him through this.
jokes.setup: Moira notices David isn't present
jokes.punchline: Patrick's observation that David is pointlessly cleaning already-clean shelves — displacement activity as coping mechanism, observed with gentle acceptance
jokes.explanation: Patrick's patient acceptance of David's coping mechanism is a warm character moment with a light comedic touch. 'Whatever it takes to get him through this' applied to cleaning already-clean shelves is gentle and well-observed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I look absolutely ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You do.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And yet...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: And yet, I'm still attracted to you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's very sweet.

jokes.text: Priority number one, get David through this! Mhmm, mhmm!
jokes.setup: Patrick says he wants to get David through the embarrassment; Moira enthusiastically agrees
jokes.punchline: Moira's performative agreement, with repeated 'mhmm,' rings hollow given she was the one who broadcast his secret
jokes.explanation: Moira's performative enthusiasm for protecting David — from the thing she caused — is a standard dramatic irony beat. Works but isn't particularly fresh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I love you. I love you so much that I'm willing to overlook the fact that you're currently dressed like a Victorian-era deep-sea diver having an asthma attack.

jokes.text: Okay, okay... so, no biggie, but my mom may have accidentally posted your little conversation from this morning over social media.
jokes.setup: Patrick and Alexis are breaking the news about the accidental livestream
jokes.punchline: 'No biggie' before revealing that Patrick's fiancé's bed-wetting has been publicly broadcast is a masterful piece of Alexis understatement
jokes.explanation: 'No biggie' as the preface to genuinely bad news is classic Alexis tone-management. The 'little conversation' euphemism is also funny. Alexis's habit of minimizing disasters she's involved in is very in-character.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, give me your phone.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Just trust me. Give it to me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, it's 11 PM. What is happening right now?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: There's something on Twitter that you don't need to see before bed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what did I do?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Nothing! You did nothing. But the internet did something, and you're not looking at it until morning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you're scaring me.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's the point. Now go to sleep.

jokes.text: Miss CherryBus, suggested I try my hand at creating a living stream.
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining how the accidental livestream happened
jokes.punchline: 'Living stream' for 'livestream' — Moira's malapropism that sounds almost intentional, as if she's naming something that is alive
jokes.explanation: 'Living stream' is a great Moira error because it's poetic — a livestream IS a living stream in some sense. Her malapropism accidentally makes more linguistic sense than the original term. This is the kind of Moira writing that rewards close attention.

jokes.text: Okay, David can never, ever find out about this. I am actually afraid that he will never sleep again.
jokes.setup: The group is conspiring to hide the livestream from David
jokes.punchline: Alexis's genuine fear that David's anxiety will lead to sleep deprivation — she knows her brother's catastrophizing well enough to predict permanent insomnia
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Alexis knows David well enough to be legitimately afraid of his potential reaction. Not a standout but consistent with character.

jokes.text: So all we need to do is keep David off the internet for like, 24 hours, or until a celebrity says something wrong, which could be any minute.
jokes.setup: The plan requires keeping David offline
jokes.punchline: The timeline of 'until a celebrity says something wrong' as a reliable expiration date for internet scandals — and 'could be any minute' as accurate commentary on celebrity behavior
jokes.explanation: Sharp media-savvy observational joke from Alexis — she understands the internet's news cycle perfectly. The phrase 'which could be any minute' is accurate and a little dark about celebrity culture. Alexis's media fluency making her the most competent person in this situation is consistently funny.

jokes.text: It's kind of cute, though. / Alexis. / Yes.
jokes.setup: Moira still hasn't taken down the post; Alexis catches her
jokes.punchline: Alexis momentarily breaks from crisis mode to admit she finds the situation 'kind of cute' — before being called back to duty
jokes.explanation: Moira's admission that she finds the accidental podcast 'cute' undercuts her supposed urgency. The two-word exchange 'Alexis.' / 'Yes.' is good economy — Moira's one-word call-to-attention and Alexis's compliant single-word response.

jokes.text: Same reason I left the button factory when I was a young man. That, and the fact that working conditions weren't exactly safe for a ten-year-old.
jokes.setup: Johnny is encouraging Stevie to pursue her dreams, relating it to his own early life
jokes.punchline: The sudden reveal that Johnny worked in a button factory as a ten-year-old — and then the further revelation that working conditions were unsafe for a child there
jokes.explanation: A genuinely surprising and dark joke embedded in a warm, sincere moment. The first half sounds like a nostalgic story; the second half reveals child labor in an unsafe factory. The deadpan way Johnny adds this biographical detail — as if it's totally normal — is very funny. Good structural surprise: the pivot from inspirational to 'it wasn't safe for a ten-year-old there' lands hard.

jokes.text: I'm gonna miss that enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Stevie says she'll think about her future while finishing her shift, having initially suggested heading home
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dry 'I'm gonna miss that enthusiasm' — deadpan acknowledgment that Stevie's brand of reluctant duty is what he appreciates
jokes.explanation: A warm, light joke that captures the Johnny-Stevie relationship. 'Enthusiasm' is used entirely ironically — Stevie just grudgingly agreed to finish her shift. The affection underneath the irony is what makes the show special.

jokes.text: I'm so glad you decided to stay. / Well, there's literally no liquid left in my body, so.
jokes.setup: David has finally returned to being present in the store; Patrick is glad
jokes.punchline: David's reason for staying is not emotional resolve but physical necessity — he's dehydrated himself through seven showers and refusing liquids
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback that pays off multiple earlier setups simultaneously — the seven showers, the untouched tea, the liquid self-deprivation. 'There's literally no liquid left in my body' is David at his most hilariously dramatic while also being medically... somewhat concerning. The 'so' at the end is a perfect trailing note. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Taking that off first thing tomorrow. Huh!
jokes.setup: David has just discovered the mattress protector is still there
jokes.punchline: His decision to deal with it 'first thing tomorrow' — accepting the plastic sheet for tonight — is a capitulation that marks his emotional arc
jokes.explanation: The 'Huh!' at the end is a perfect character sound — resigned acceptance. David's arc from 'I will never see you again' to 'I'll deal with the plastic sheet tomorrow' is the quiet emotional through-line of the episode.

jokes.text: I wonder how long my mom lasted on the internets. / Not long, I don't think.
jokes.setup: The conspiracy to hide the livestream from David has been successful — he has no idea
jokes.punchline: David's innocent question about Moira's social media tenure while Patrick knows the full scope of the disaster
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony — the audience knows Patrick is drastically underplaying 'not long, I don't think' while navigating a delicate truth. 'The internets' (plural) from David is a funny casual tech malapropism.

jokes.text: You know, you were smart to get off of there. / Mhmm. I was curious to see what kinds of things she was posting throughout the day, though. / Yeah, nothing of note.
jokes.setup: David is blissfully unaware; Patrick is managing the truth in real-time
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'nothing of note' for what was apparently an entire podcast about David's bed-wetting and a viral incident
jokes.explanation: 'Nothing of note' as a summary of 'your most embarrassing secret was broadcast to thousands of people' is a good understatement joke. Patrick's patient, calm management of this situation is consistent and sweet.

jokes.text: Hey, let's talk about something else. Sure, um... what's the thread count on this plastic?
jokes.setup: Patrick deflects from the social media topic; David looks for a new subject
jokes.punchline: David immediately pivots to the mattress protector — his mind goes straight to the plastic sheet as a topic of conversation, suggesting it's still bothering him
jokes.explanation: 'What's the thread count on this plastic?' is a great line — David asking a question about plastic in the language of luxury bedding (thread counts) reveals both his continued preoccupation with the sheet and his instinct to apply high-end metrics to everything. Highly character-specific and funny.

jokes.text: Okay, would it help if I were to share something private and embarrassing with you?
jokes.setup: David is still somewhat withdrawn; Patrick wants to help him feel less alone in his embarrassment
jokes.punchline: The formal phrasing 'would it help if I were to share...' for what's about to be a vulnerable moment is simultaneously sweet and slightly clinical
jokes.explanation: The setup for the mouthguard reveal — functions as dramatic anticipation. Patrick's formal phrasing is mildly amusing but mostly serves the scene's emotional function.

jokes.text: Okay, I only put this in when you're not around. [Patrick removes a mouthguard]
jokes.setup: Patrick said he'd share something embarrassing; he produces a mouthguard from his mouth
jokes.punchline: Patrick only wears his mouthguard when David isn't there — implying he's been concealing this from the man he's about to marry
jokes.explanation: The visual reveal of the mouthguard is the punchline — Patrick, who has been the composed, dignified foil all episode, has his own small embarrassing secret. The detail that he hides it from David adds sweetness: they're both performing for each other, and they're about to stop. Rewatch bonus for the visual and the thematic resonance.

jokes.text: Also, you look very beautiful. / Aw, thank you.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just removed a mouthguard; David says there's nothing embarrassing about it
jokes.punchline: David calling Patrick 'very beautiful' with a mouthguard in his hand — the compliment is sincere but absurdly timed
jokes.explanation: David's sincere, immediate 'you look very beautiful' after a mouthguard reveal is funny because of its context — it's a genuine compliment, not ironic, which is somehow funnier. Patrick's simple 'Aw, thank you' accepts it completely. The warmth between them in this moment is both sweet and slightly absurd.

jokes.text: How about... now? [Patrick puts on a large nose breathing apparatus] / My God! This is my nose thing, and it just helps me breathe better.
jokes.setup: Patrick has been revealing embarrassing nighttime accessories; David appreciated the mouthguard
jokes.punchline: Patrick produces a nose breathing device (presumably a CPAP or nasal dilator strip apparatus) — a larger, more elaborate piece of medical bedtime equipment
jokes.explanation: The escalation from mouthguard to nose device is perfect structure — each reveal gets bigger and more medically elaborate. 'My God!' from David is a great reaction beat. The scene is mirroring the opening (medical/bodily embarrassment in bed) but now as intimacy rather than shame. Thematically satisfying comedy.

jokes.text: I have never been more attracted to you. / Mm, same.
jokes.setup: Patrick is now wearing both a mouthguard and a nose breathing device
jokes.punchline: Both declare maximum attraction at what is objectively a very unglamorous moment — the sincerity is the joke
jokes.explanation: A beautiful payoff to the entire episode arc. The show's thesis about mature love — that true intimacy means being attracted to each other in the undignified moments — is delivered as a funny line. 'I have never been more attracted to you' said to someone in nose gear and a mouthguard, and meaning it, is what separates Schitt's Creek from lesser shows. 'Mm, same' from Patrick is perfect economy. This is the episode's emotional and comedic peak together.

jokes.text: Ronnie's texting me a link to something? / Okay, put it away, David. It's bedtime.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick have reached a sweet, intimate moment; David's phone buzzes
jokes.punchline: Patrick's immediate 'put it away, it's bedtime' — he knows exactly what that link is (the viral livestream) and intercepts it smoothly
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing joke is structurally perfect. The threat of the secret being exposed — which has been the B-plot all episode — arrives at the final moment, and Patrick kills it with three words: 'put it away.' His timing, knowing immediately what the link is without looking, demonstrates both his knowledge of the situation and his commitment to protecting David. The sweet scene continues uninterrupted, the disaster averted by his quiet competence. Great button for the episode — funny, character-revealing, and narratively satisfying simultaneously.

tmdb_overview: David is embarrassed by a childhood issue that resurfaces, and only spirals further when Patrick is so understanding. Johnny, Stevie and Roland attend a viewing for a nearby motel. Alexis helps Moira join social media.
insight: Absurdist escalation drives 64 jokes in 40 minutes—Schitt's Creek at peak density.
